annotations.label: Random variable (informal)

anchor.snippet: A random variable (also called random quantity

annotations.note: Informal prose description with no specific Mathlib counterpart.

annotations.label: Random variable (measure-theoretic, lead)

anchor.snippet: In the formal mathematical language of measure theory

annotations.note: Mathlib models a random variable as a `Measurable` function from a probability space.

annotations.label: Random variate

anchor.snippet: A random variate is a particular outcome or realization of a random variable.

annotations.note: A realization `X ω` has no dedicated Mathlib name.

annotations.label: Random variable as measurable function

anchor.snippet: is a measurable function

annotations.note: `Measurable` is the exact measure-theoretic notion used.

annotations.label: Discrete random variable

anchor.snippet: the random variable is called a discrete random variable

annotations.note: Discrete distributions are captured by `PMF`, but `Mathlib` has no class `IsDiscreteRandomVariable`.

annotations.label: Continuous random variable

anchor.snippet: is called a continuous random variable

annotations.note: Absolutely continuous random variables are formalized via `HasPDF`; the looser "continuous CDF" notion is not separately named.

annotations.label: Absolutely continuous random variable

anchor.snippet: In the special case that it is absolutely continuous

annotations.note: `HasPDF X ℙ μ` says `(map X ℙ) ≪ μ` with a density.

annotations.label: Cumulative distribution function

anchor.snippet: Any random variable can be described by its cumulative distribution function

annotations.note: `cdf μ : StieltjesFunction ℝ` is the CDF of a measure on ℝ.

annotations.label: Random element

anchor.snippet: Thus one can consider random elements of other sets

annotations.note: Random elements valued in arbitrary measurable spaces are just `Measurable` maps in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Probability distribution of a random variable

anchor.snippet: Recording all these probabilities of outputs of a random variable

annotations.note: The law of `X` is `Measure.map X ℙ`, and `ProbabilityTheory.HasLaw` packages this.

annotations.label: Discrete distribution function

anchor.snippet: is a discrete distribution function

annotations.note: The step-function CDF of a discrete law is not a named decl; `PMF` is the related object.

annotations.label: Person's height as random variable

anchor.snippet: An example of a random variable may be the person's height.

annotations.note: Informal real-world example.

annotations.label: Number of children as discrete random variable

anchor.snippet: Another random variable may be the person's number of children

annotations.note: Informal real-world example.

annotations.label: Coin toss random variable

anchor.snippet: The possible outcomes for one coin toss can be described by the sample space

annotations.note: Informal example; no specific Mathlib decl about a coin-toss random variable.

anchors.snippet: The possible outcomes for one coin toss can be described by the sample space

annotations.label: Dice roll random variable

anchor.snippet: A random variable can also be used to describe the process of rolling dice

annotations.note: Informal example; no specific Mathlib decl.

anchors.snippet: A random variable can also be used to describe the process of rolling dice

annotations.label: Continuous random variable (formal)

anchor.snippet: Formally, a continuous random variable is a random variable whose cumulative distribution function is continuous everywhere

annotations.note: Mathlib has CDFs but no named class for "CDF is everywhere continuous".

annotations.label: Spinner as continuous random variable

anchor.snippet: An example of a continuous random variable would be one based on a spinner

annotations.note: Informal example.

annotations.label: Continuous uniform random variable (CURV)

anchor.snippet: is called a " continuous uniform random variable" (CURV)

annotations.note: Uniform distributions are formalized in `Mathlib.Probability.Distributions.Uniform`.

anchors.snippet: is called a " continuous uniform random variable" (CURV)

annotations.label: Mixed random variable

anchor.snippet: A mixed random variable is a random variable whose cumulative distribution function is neither discrete nor everywhere-continuous

annotations.note: No dedicated "mixed random variable" concept in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Coin-and-spinner mixed random variable

anchor.snippet: An example of a random variable of mixed type would be based on an experiment where a coin is flipped

annotations.note: Informal example.

annotations.label: Lebesgue decomposition of distributions on the real line

anchor.snippet: every probability distribution on the real line is a mixture of discrete part, singular part, and an absolutely continuous part

annotations.note: Mathlib's Lebesgue decomposition splits into a.c. and singular parts but does not further decompose the singular part into discrete + singular-continuous.

annotations.label: Measure-theoretic random variable

anchor.snippet: Then an

annotations.note: A measurable function between measurable spaces is `Measurable`.

annotations.label: E-valued random variable (Borel)

anchor.snippet: In such case the

annotations.note: An `E`-valued random variable is `Measurable X` with `[MeasurableSpace E] [BorelSpace E]`.

annotations.label: Real-valued random variable

anchor.snippet: the function

annotations.note: Real-valued random variables are `Measurable X : Ω → ℝ`.

annotations.label: Expected value / first moment

anchor.snippet: This is captured by the mathematical concept of expected value of a random variable

annotations.note: Expectation is the Bochner integral `∫ ω, X ω ∂ℙ`; also `ProbabilityTheory.moment X 1 μ`.

annotations.label: Generalised problem of moments

anchor.snippet: Mathematically, this is known as the (generalised) problem of moments

annotations.note: The generalized moment problem is not formalized as such.

annotations.label: Function of a random variable

anchor.snippet: A new random variable

annotations.note: `g ∘ X` is realised by `Measurable.comp` when `g` is measurable.

annotations.label: CDF of g(X) for invertible monotone g

anchor.snippet: If function

annotations.note: No named lemma for `F_{g(X)} = F_X ∘ g⁻¹` in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Density transformation under invertible differentiable g

anchor.snippet: assuming also differentiability , the relation between the probability density functions can be found by differentiating both sides

annotations.note: Change-of-variables / Jacobian formulas exist but no named pdf-transformation result for `g(X)`.

annotations.label: Density transformation with countably many roots

anchor.snippet: If there is no invertibility of

annotations.note: The multi-branch density transformation formula is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Composition of measurable function with random variable is a random variable

anchor.snippet: since the composition of measurable functions is also measurable

annotations.note: `Measurable.comp` gives composition of measurable functions is measurable.

annotations.label: Example 1: function of a continuous random variable

anchor.snippet: be a real-valued, continuous random variable

annotations.note: Worked example with no specific Mathlib decl.

annotations.label: Example 2: exponential distribution via transformation

anchor.snippet: which is the cumulative distribution function (CDF) of an exponential distribution

annotations.note: Exponential distribution is defined, but this specific transformation example is not formalized.

annotations.label: Example 3: chi-squared from standard normal

anchor.snippet: This is a chi-squared distribution with one degree of freedom

annotations.note: Chi-squared distribution not present as a named distribution in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Example 4: noncentral chi-squared from normal

anchor.snippet: This is a noncentral chi-squared distribution with one degree of freedom

annotations.note: Noncentral chi-squared distribution is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Distribution of sum of independent random variables is convolution

anchor.snippet: The probability distribution of the sum of two independent random variables is the convolution of each of their distributions.

annotations.note: The additive version follows via `to_additive` from `IndepFun.map_mul_eq_map_mconv_map`.

annotations.label: Probability distributions form a convex set

anchor.snippet: they are closed under convex combination

annotations.note: No explicit statement that `IsProbabilityMeasure` is closed under convex combinations as a convex space.

annotations.label: Equality in distribution

anchor.snippet: random variables X and Y are equal in distribution

annotations.note: `IdentDistrib f g μ ν` says `map f μ = map g ν`.

annotations.label: Equal MGFs imply same distribution

anchor.snippet: Two random variables having equal moment generating functions have the same distribution.

annotations.note: Comments in `Mathlib.Probability.Moments.ComplexMGF` explicitly list this as a TODO.

annotations.label: Almost sure equality

anchor.snippet: Two random variables X and Y are equal almost surely

annotations.note: Written `X =ᵐ[μ] Y`, i.e. `Filter.EventuallyEq (ae μ) X Y`.

annotations.label: Equality of random variables

anchor.snippet: the two random variables X and Y are equal if they are equal as functions on their measurable space

annotations.note: Pointwise equality of `Ω → E` functions is just `Eq` (or `funext`).
